body_md: Use a nearest-centroid classifier on synthetic two-Gaussian blobs rather than a learned model for phase 1: the worst-case perturbation direction is known in closed form, so measured accuracies can be validated against theory before trusting the harness on learned models.

Single seed (7) for phase 1 is acceptable because every measured number has a closed-form prediction to check against; multi-seed variance becomes mandatory in the learned-model phase (see ideation.md evaluation plan).
At equal L2 budget, targeted noise along the inter-class axis degrades accuracy far more than random isotropic noise, and the gap grows with dimension (random effective shift ~ eps/sqrt(d)).
Clean baseline: acc=0.835 on the held-out 600 (run 1), matching the closed-form prediction Phi(1)~=0.841 within sampling error (se~=0.015). Harness validated.

Random isotropic noise is nearly harmless at d=32: acc=0.835 at eps=0.5 (run 2) and 0.830 at eps=1.0 (run 3), i.e. a 0.5-point drop at the full budget - random directions waste the budget in the d-1 irrelevant dimensions, and the axis component is symmetric so first-order effects cancel.

Targeted noise at equal budget: acc=0.670 at eps=0.5 (run 4, predicted Phi(0.5)~=0.691) and 0.462 at eps=1.0 (run 5, predicted Phi(0)=0.5, slightly below due to estimated-centroid error). Drop of 37.3 points vs 0.5 points for random at eps=1.0 - a ~75x gap.

Run 5 lands slightly BELOW chance-boundary prediction (0.462 < 0.5): the attack direction is computed from ESTIMATED centroids, which adds a small systematic push past the true boundary. Worth a note in the paper; not a bug.

Reframing (from Julian): a completed thesis on targeted noise injection already exists. This project is the staged REPRODUCTION of that thesis - re-derive each mechanism in small, verifiable steps (one experiment per mechanism claim, every number from a recorded run), then distill into a paper. The phase-1 synthetic harness stands as harness validation only; the experiment DAG will be restructured around the thesis's actual mechanism claims once the thesis is ingested into library/ (reref add <thesis.pdf>) and its claims extracted.
Does the targeted-vs-random gap actually grow as eps/sqrt(d) predicts? Needs 004-dimension-sweep.
Phase-2 result is a good calibration; before the paper, show the same protocol on a LEARNED boundary (logistic regression at minimum) or reviewers will read it as a tautology.
Does the gap survive when the targeted direction must be ESTIMATED from a learned model, rather than known in closed form? This is the claim that matters in practice (thesis phase 2).

Evaluation design (phase 1, designed before building): dataset tni-blobs-d32 (hash-pinned, d=32, n=1200, centers +-1.0, sigma=1.0, seed 7; closed-form clean acc Phi(1)~=0.841); baselines = clean accuracy and random-noise accuracy at the same eps; metrics acc/drop per eps in {0.5, 1.0} plus a full eps-sweep curve artifact per run.
Test
Test

Ideation: what is the thesis, and what evidence would support it? State the thesis and contributions as claims, the evaluation design as a decision entry (designed before building), open risks as questions, and position against prior art by citing papers. The plan lives in this graph — not in a file.

Direction: pursue the fidelity-requirements study with a Blender-native event rendering plugin as the released artifact, not the headline. July 2026 prior-art sweep: Blender-native niche open (EVIS took Isaac Sim; only a 2-star hobby repo in Blender), HDR-vs-8bit quantization ablation unclaimed in the literature, MC-noise statistical treatments exist (ICIP23/ICCP24/EventTracer) but not in Cycles. Blender-to-v2e-as-headline lands at workshop tier (SynSacc, UEOF at WACV-W 2026).

8-bit sRGB quantization before video-to-event conversion measurably degrades downstream perception versus linear HDR float input, and the effect is largest for faint sub-pixel structures (snowflakes at low DN) where log-intensity quantization is coarsest.

How much downstream-perception accuracy does motion-vector warp interpolation lose on sub-pixel particle motion (flake disocclusions) compared to brute-force high-rate rendering, at what render-cost saving?
Can event generation from Cycles HDR buffers with MC-noise-aware thresholding be implemented as a pure bpy add-on, or does adaptive temporal sampling require Cycles C++ changes?
Which downstream instrument (RVT detection / ESS segmentation from the thesis harness) is most sensitive to event-simulation fidelity axes, and at what dataset scale do differences become measurable?

Corpus coverage: 45+ papers ingested (research-sweep + thesis bib). Paywalled/no-OA skips: zhang2021snowkitti, ruan2024distill, delaney2025evaluating, tomy2022fusing, locatelli1974; C3I microsaccade and JSTASR are DOI-metadata-only (no full text, not span-citable). BlinkSim EXR/in-process-emulator facts are code-level (github zju3dv/blink_sim), not stated in the BlinkFlow paper text - cite the repo, not the paper, for those.
